34: ephphatha — Ephphatha — another Aramaic preservation (cf. 5:41); the word itself becomes performative: Jesus says be opened and the ears open. Mogilalos (v.32, speaking with difficulty or mute) is the LXX word in Isa 35:6 (the tongue of the mute will sing for joy) — Mark uses the Isaiah-term for the man healed, signaling that the Isaianic healing-sign is being enacted. The detail of Jesus's sighing (estenaxen) before the healing is a characteristic Markan note of Jesus's genuine human emotion and the weight he carries in the healing-mission.
1: hoi Pharisaioi kai tines tōn grammateōn elthontes apo Hierosolymōn — Pharisees and some of the scribes having come from Jerusalem — the delegation from the capital marks an escalation: this is not local scribal scrutiny (as in 2:6; 3:22) but an official investigation mounted from Jerusalem. The combination of Pharisees (lay pietists concerned with halakhic observance) and scribes (legal scholars and Torah-interpreters) constitutes a joint inquiry into the movement's practices.
2: koinais chersin, tout' estin aniptois — with common hands, that is, unwashed — koinos (common, defiled, ritually impure) is the LXX and NT technical term for the state of ritual impurity; the parenthetical "that is, unwashed" (tout' estin aniptois) is Mark's aside explaining Jewish purity practice to Gentile readers unfamiliar with the custom. The accusation is halakhic non-compliance, not hygiene.
3: pugmē — the term is lexically debated: (1) "with a fist" = scrubbing vigorously with a cupped hand; (2) "up to the wrist/elbow" (a few MSS support a distance-measure reading). UBS5/NA28 reads pugmē. Whatever the specific gesture, the parenthetical vv.3–4 is a Markan explanatory aside for non-Jewish readers, establishing the traditional practice before Jesus's ruling on it.
4: kai apo agoras ean mē baptisōntai ouk esthiousin — and from the marketplace unless they immerse they do not eat — baptizō (to dip, immerse) here refers to ritual immersion after contact with potentially impure persons in the marketplace. The additional listed practices — immersing cups, pitchers, and copper vessels, and klinōn (dining couches) in some manuscripts — show how pervasively ritual purity governed daily domestic life. Defilement could pass through objects, not only persons.
5: dia ti ou peripatousin hoi mathētai sou kata tēn paradosin tōn presbyterōn — why do your disciples not walk according to the tradition of the elders — paradosis (tradition, what is handed over/passed down — from paradidonai) is the disputed term. The scribes frame the issue as violation of ancestral custom rather than Torah itself. Jesus's response will reverse the frame: it is the tradition that violates Torah.
6: ho de eipen autois, Kalōs eprophēteusen Hsaias peri hymōn tōn hypokritōn — and he said to them: rightly Isaiah prophesied about you hypocrites — hypokritēs (actor wearing a mask, stage-performer) is the word for theatrical performance in Greek; those who speak lines not their own character. The citation follows: Isaiah 29:13 LXX. houtos ho laos tois cheilesin me tima, hē de kardia autōn porrō apechei ap' emou — this people honors me with lips but their heart is far from me — the lips/heart contrast is the prophetic anatomy of empty religion: external speech without inward reality.
7: matēn de sebontai me, didaskontes didaskalias entalmata anthrōpōn — they worship me in vain, teaching as doctrines human commandments — matēn (in vain, without purpose, to no effect — the worship achieves nothing). The LXX version of Isaiah 29:13 is actually stronger at this point than the Hebrew MT, using entalmata anthrōpōn (human commandments/injunctions) to characterize the teaching. The human origin of the tradition (anthrōpōn) is contrasted throughout with the divine origin of the law (entolē tou theou in vv.8–9).
8: aphentes tēn entolēn tou theou krateite tēn paradosin tōn anthrōpōn — letting go of the commandment of God you hold the tradition of men — entolē (commandment, from entellomai = to give authoritative command) vs. paradosis (tradition, secondary transmission). The opposition is structural: divine commandment released, human tradition grasped. The priority is inverted: what God directly commanded is treated as secondary to what the elders subsequently taught.
9: kalōs atheteite tēn entolēn tou theou hina tēn paradosin hymōn stēsēte — rightly you set aside the commandment of God in order to establish your tradition — kalōs is bitterly ironic: "nicely/skillfully you do it." atheteō (to set aside, declare void — from a- + tithēmi = to un-place, unmake the position of) is the legal term for rendering a ruling or obligation invalid. The purpose clause hina ... stēsēte (in order that you might establish) exposes the implicit logic: tradition is upheld at Torah's expense.
10: Mōysēs gar eipen, Tima ton patera sou kai tēn mētera sou — for Moses said: Honor your father and your mother — the Moses-said formula grounds the commandment in Sinai authority (Exod 20:12; Deut 5:16). The second citation — "the one who curses father or mother, let him die" (Exod 21:17; Lev 20:9) — is the capital-penalty provision that underlines how seriously Torah treats parental honor. Jesus establishes the Torah's own severity before exposing the tradition's circumvention of it.
11: korban, ho estin dōron — Korban, which is a gift — korban is the Aramaic loanword for a gift dedicated to the temple treasury (Hebrew qorban, from qarab = to draw near, to offer). Once resources were declared korban — dedicated to God — they could not legally be redirected, even to support parents in material need. The tradition thus created a halakhically valid mechanism by which a son could exempt himself from financial parental support (ou mē ōphelēthēs ex emou = you will not be benefited from me) by invoking a temple-dedication vow.
12: ouketi aphiete auton ouden poiēsai tō patri ē tē mētri — you no longer permit him to do anything for his father or mother — the legal consequence: the korban-declaration extinguishes all subsequent obligation to parents. The tradition has not merely supplemented Torah; it has generated a loophole that makes Torah compliance impossible once the vow is made. Jesus's case study concretizes the abstract charge of v.8–9 with a specific mechanism.
13: akyrountes ton logon tou theou tē paradosei hymōn hē paredōkate — nullifying the word of God by your tradition which you have handed on — akyrouō (to make without authority, to annul — from a- + kyrios = making the Lord's ruling invalid) is the antonym of kyroō (to confirm, validate). The tradition (paradosis) has accumulated institutional authority sufficient to effectively override Torah. "And many such things like this you do" — the korban case is one instance of a systemic pattern, not an isolated exception.
14: kai proskalesamenos palin ton ochlon elegen autois — and calling the crowd to himself again he said — Jesus widens the audience from the Pharisees/scribes (vv.1–13) to the crowd. The teaching that follows is addressed publicly. Akouete mou pantes kai suniete — hear me all and understand — the double imperative with pantes (all) marks this as a universal public declaration, not a private rulings addressed only to the questioning party.
15: ouden estin exōthen tou anthrōpou eisporeuomenon eis auton ho dynatai koinōsai auton — nothing outside the person entering into him is able to defile him — koinoō (to make common, to render ritually impure) is the verb throughout the pericope. The principle reverses the Levitical purity paradigm: defilement moves outward from the person, not inward from outside. The implication for the food-law controversy is radical: what is eaten (external to the person) cannot be the source of defilement.
16: Many manuscripts include ei tis echei ōta akouein akouetō — if anyone has ears to hear, let him hear — as v.16, an interpolation from 4:9,23. UBS5/NA28 brackets or omits it. If original, it would signal this teaching as parabolic in character, requiring inner-ear understanding rather than surface-level hearing — consistent with the private explanation that follows in vv.17–23.
17: kai hote eisēlthen eis oikon apo tou ochlou, epērōtōn auton hoi mathētai autou tēn parabolēn — and when he entered a house away from the crowd his disciples asked him about the parable — the house/crowd spatial division recurs (cf. 4:10; 7:24; 9:28; 10:10): private explanation follows public proclamation. parabolē is used broadly for any dark or enigmatic saying, not only narrative parables. Outōs kai hymeis asyntetoi este — are you also without understanding — asynetos (without comprehension — from a- + syniēmi = failing to put it together) is the word for those who cannot assemble the evidence into understanding; cf. 8:17, 21.
18: ou noeite hoti pan to exōthen eisporeuomenon eis ton anthrōpon ou dynatai auton koinōsai — do you not understand that nothing entering the person from outside is able to defile him — noeō (to perceive with the mind, to grasp intellectually — from nous) is paired with the negative ou: the disciples' cognitive failure is named. The re-statement of v.15 is slightly expanded with the digestive explanation that follows in v.19: what enters goes into the stomach, not the heart (kardia), and is expelled — the physical process cannot touch the moral centre.
19: hoti ouk eisporeuetai autou eis tēn kardian all' eis tēn koilian, kai eis ton aphedrōna ekporeuetai, katharizōn panta ta brōmata — because it does not enter his heart but his stomach and goes out into the latrine, cleansing all foods — the parenthetical katharizōn panta ta brōmata (cleansing/declaring clean all foods) is Mark's editorial aside identifying the ruling implication of Jesus's teaching. This phrase is absent from Matthew's parallel (15:17). It is the most significant halakhic ruling in the Gospel traditions, effectively overturning the Levitical dietary laws (Lev 11), and anticipates the vision in Acts 10:15 and Paul's teaching in Romans 14. The subject of katharizōn is disputed: the grammatically nearest antecedent is Jesus, making this a Markan declaration that Jesus declared all foods clean.
20: to de ek tou anthrōpou ekporeuomenon, ekeino koinoi ton anthrōpon — but what comes out of the person, that defiles the person — the inverted principle is stated explicitly: defilement has a single source, the human interior. The list that follows (vv.21–22) catalogs what the interior produces. The contrast with Levitical purity (which assigned defilement to external objects, foods, bodily discharges) is complete: for Jesus, the heart is the source, not the skin or stomach.
21: esōthen gar ek tēs kardias tōn anthrōpōn hoi dialogismoi hoi kakoi ekporeuontai — for from within, from the heart of persons, the evil reasonings come out — dialogismos (reasoning, deliberation — from dialogizomai = to reason through; in NT consistently the internal deliberation that produces moral conclusions) is the first item: cognitive evil, the mind's disposition. The following list of 6 plural nouns (fornications, thefts, murders, adulteries, covetings, wickednesses) and 6 singular nouns (deceit, sensuality, evil eye, slander, arrogance, folly) is a catalog of inward moral corruption, not ritual violation.
22: aselgeia — sensuality, licentiousness (the absence of moral restraint); ophthalmos ponēros — evil eye — in Semitic idiom, the evil eye is envy and miserliness (Matt 6:23; Prov 23:6 LXX), not an occult gaze; blasphēmia — slander, defamation (of persons as well as God); hyperēphania — arrogance, haughtiness (from hyper + phainomai = appearing above others — the pride of social superiority); aphrosynē — folly, senselessness (the moral failure of not acknowledging God). The list ends with the foolishness that Proverbs names as the foundation of all moral failure.
23: panta tauta ta ponēra esōthen ekporeuetai kai koinoi ton anthrōpon — all these evil things come out from within and defile the person — the concluding summary applies koinoō (defile) to the list. The pericope's argument is complete: the tradition's application of koinōsis to unwashed hands is misapplied; the real defilement comes from the moral interior, not from physical contact with food or persons.
24: kai ekeithen anastas apēlthen eis ta horia Tyrou — and rising from there he departed into the regions of Tyre — Tyre is on the Phoenician coast approximately 35 miles northwest of Capernaum — Gentile territory entirely outside Jewish Galilee. kai eiselthōn eis oikian oudena ēthelen gnōnai, kai ouk ēdynasthē lathein — and entering a house he wanted no one to know, and he was unable to escape notice — the Messianic Secret maintained: private residence, no-announcement intention. But his reputation precedes him even into Gentile regions; the attempt at concealment fails immediately.
25: all' eutheōs akousasa gynē peri autou, hēs eichen to thygatrion autēs pneuma akatharton — but immediately a woman whose little daughter had an unclean spirit, having heard about him — eutheōs again; the woman's approach is immediate upon hearing. thygatrion (little daughter — diminutive of thygatēr) is the affectionate parental form, as with Jairus's daughter (5:23). prosepesen pros tous podas autou (fell at his feet) — the posture of extreme supplication, as with Jairus (5:22) and the Gerasene man (5:33). The same gesture crosses cultural boundaries.
26: hē de gynē ēn Hellēnis, Syrophoinikissa tō genei — the woman was Greek/Gentile, Syrophoenician by race — Hellēnis (Hellenist — Greek-speaking, Gentile by culture) identifies her cultural orientation; Syrophoinikissa tō genei (Syrophoenician by race/descent) specifies ethnic origin — Phoenician Syria as distinct from Libyan Phoenicia. She is doubly marked as non-Jewish, and her request is that Jesus cast the demon out of her daughter. The initial response Jesus gives (v.27) will test the mission-boundary question directly.
27: aphes prōton chortasthēnai ta tekna — let the children first be satisfied/filled — prōton (first) is the key word: it acknowledges mission-priority (Israel before Gentiles) without making that priority absolute or eternal. chortazō (to be filled, satisfied — used for feeding animals and, in the Beatitude, those who hunger) matches the feeding-miracle language. ou gar kalon estin labein ton arton tōn teknōn kai tois kynarois balein — for it is not good to take the children's bread and throw it to the dogs — kynarion (little dog, household dog — diminutive) is a softened form; kyōn (the street-dog insult) is not used. The children-dogs metaphor maps covenant Israel (tekna) vs. Gentiles (kynaria) with the ordering acknowledged but not the exclusion made permanent.
28: kyrie, kai ta kynarion hypokato tēs trapezēs esthiousin apo tōn psichōn tōn paidōn — Lord, even the little dogs under the table eat from the children's crumbs — kyrie (Lord/Master) is her address, acknowledging his authority. The woman accepts the image and works within its logic: she does not claim the children's portion but only the psichōn (crumbs, fragments — the scraps that fall from the table). The reply is neither an argument against the ordering nor a rejection of it; it works within the metaphor to find space for Gentile participation at its margins. The theological brilliance is that she claims the overflow of the children's blessing, not the blessing itself.
29: dia touton ton logon hypage — because of this word/saying, go — the woman's logos (word, response, saying) is what Jesus names as the reason for the healing. In Matthew's parallel (15:28) it is her faith (pistis) that is named; in Mark it is the word she spoke. The healing is pronounced before any physical contact or return home: Jesus declares the demon departed (exelēlythen to daimonion ek tēs thygatros sou = the demon has gone out from your daughter) at a distance and in the past tense. The departure has already occurred.
30: kai apelthousa eis ton oikon autēs heuren to paidion beblēmenon epi tēn klinēn kai to daimonion exelēlythos — going to her house she found the child lying on the bed and the demon gone — both participles are perfects: beblēmenon (having been cast/laid — perfect passive of ballō) and exelēlythos (having gone out — perfect active of exerchomai). The perfect tenses indicate completed states at the time of discovery — the healing had already occurred, precisely when Jesus's word was spoken. The physical verification confirms the remotely-spoken pronouncement.
31: kai palin exelthōn ek tōn horiōn Tyrou ēlthen dia Sidōnos eis tēn thalassan tēs Galilaias ana meson tōn horiōn Dekapoleōs — and leaving the region of Tyre he came through Sidon to the Sea of Galilee through the middle of the region of the Decapolis — the route is geographically unusual: Sidon is north of Tyre, making the path north-then-south before arriving at Galilee via the eastern Decapolis. Some scholars have proposed a geographical error; others read it as an extended Gentile mission circuit. The Decapolis (ana meson = through the middle) is where the Gerasene had been sent as missionary (5:20): Jesus moves through the territory that man had evangelized.
32: kai pherosin autō kōphon kai mogillalon kai parakalosin auton hina epithē autō tēn cheira — and they brought to him a deaf man speaking with difficulty and they begged him to lay his hand on him — kōphos (deaf/mute — Greek uses one word for both) describes the primary impairment; mogilalos (speaking with difficulty, barely able to speak — from mogis = scarcely + laleō = speak) is a rare word that appears in the Greek OT (LXX) at Isaiah 35:6: "the tongue of the mute (mogilalōn) will shout for joy." Mark's use of the Isaiah-vocabulary signals that the messianic healing-sign of the Isaianic new exodus is being enacted.
33: kai apolabomenos auton apo tou ochlou kat' idian — taking him apart from the crowd privately — the withdrawal from public space for private healing recurs (5:40; 8:23): the healing is not a public performance. The physical method: (1) ebalen tous daktylous autou eis ta ōta autou (put his fingers into the man's ears); (2) ptysas hēpsato tēs glōssēs autou (having spat he touched his tongue) — spittle used as a healing medium, consistent with 8:23. The physical contact with the affected organs is characteristic of Mark's healing accounts; it is not magic but the embodied encounter of divine healing-power with human impairment.
35: kai eutheōs diēnoichthēsan autou hai akoai, kai elythē ho desmos tēs glōssēs autou, kai elalei orthōs — and immediately his hearing was opened, and the bond of his tongue was loosed, and he spoke plainly — eutheōs again; the command was immediately effective. diēnoichthēsan (were opened — same verb-root as ephphatha). desmos (bond, restraint — what bound the tongue is now loosed) is released: elythē (was loosed, untied — aorist passive of lyō). orthōs (rightly, plainly, correctly — the adverb of straight/right) describes the quality of restored speech: not simply audible but properly formed.
36: kai diestellato autois hina mēdeni legōsin — and he commanded them to tell no one — the Messianic Secret command (cf. 1:44; 5:43; 7:36); this is the seventh silence-command in Mark. Hoson de autois diestelleto, autoi mallon perissoteron ekērysson — but the more he commanded them the more abundantly they proclaimed it — the paradox intensifies: the command is categorical, the crowd's response is the inverse. perissoteron (more abundantly, excessively) — the more he prohibited, the more emphatically they proclaimed. The Messianic Secret cannot be contained by human command.
37: kalōs panta pepoiēken, kai tous kōphous poiei akouein kai tous alalous lalein — he has done all things well, and the deaf he makes hear and the mute speak — kalōs panta pepoiēken (he has done all things well — perfect tense: the completed result stands) is widely recognized as an echo of Genesis 1:31 LXX: kai eiden ho theos ta panta hosa epoiēsen, kai idou kala lian (God saw everything he had made and behold it was very good). The crowd's acclamation of the healer uses the creation-completion formula, identifying Jesus's restoration-work as the new creation. The specific healings named — deaf hearing, mute speaking — are the Isaiah 35:5–6 messianic signs, completing the intertextual web from v.32.
